title: Canola
prose: FERTILIZATION In order to obtain a better understanding of the nutritional requirements of canola, it is important to note the following comparative figures of the removal of the most important nutrients by canola and wheat. Table 5.5.1.1. Removal of the most important nutrients by canola compared to wheat (kg ha -1 removed by 1 ton of grain) Therefore, per ton of grain produced, canola removes many more nutrients from the soil than wheat, and its fertilization requirements will therefore also be higher. To make a meaningful fertilization recommendation for canola, a full soil analysis is required, including the sulphur content, carbon percentage and stone fraction. Nitrogen As in the case of wheat, rainfall, crop rotation, soil texture, carbon content, planting date, method of establishment and the yield target should be taken into account when the nitrogen requirement is determined. Nitrogen at crop establishment Preferably use nitrate-based nitrogen. In the case of urea, it should be placed away from the seed. Planter (band-placed at seeding) â€“ 20 kg N ha -1 when sowing in 25 cm row widths. Scale down to 15â€“18 kg N ha -1 for wider rows. Broadcast â€“ not more than 30 kg N ha -1 at sowing where rainfall is <350 mm. Maximum of 40 kg N ha -1 at sowing where rainfall is >350 mm. Nitrogen topdressing The distribution of nitrogen fertilizers during the growth period of canola requires special attention. In general, two topdressings are recommended. The first topdressing must be applied 30 to 40 days after emergence. On heavier soils, it is recommended that at least 65% of the total topdressing should already be applied 30 days after emergence. On lighter soils, which tend to leach easily, two equal topdressings are recommended, i.e. at 30 days after emergence and at the onset of stem elongation. In the case of above-normal rainfall 60 to 70 days after emergence, the second topdressing should be increased accordingly. If silage canola is being produced, it is recommended that 40 kg N ha -1 be broadcast when sowing, and 50 to 60 kg N ha -1 be given as topdressing 30 to 40 days after emergence. Where canola is grown under irrigation as part of crop rotation systems that include soya (see Chapter 3), it requires approximately 160â€“180 kg N ha -1 (depending on soil properties and yield target), which should be applied as follows: Table 5.5.1.2. Nitrogen fertilization guidelines for canola Note: * Grazing in which grasses are controlled. ** Higher value applies to lighter soils. *** Includes minimum and no-tillage. Where the crop rotation system does not include any legumes, an additional 20 kg N ha -1 should be considered during the flowering stage. Phosphorus Phosphorus fulfils an important function as carrier (transporter) of energy in plants, but also forms part of the phospholipids contained in cell membranes. Even though phosphorus is an essential macro-nutrient, smaller quantities are required compared to nitrogen. Phosphorus-deficient plants are mainly characterised by limited vegetative growth and sometimes also red or purple discolouration. However, these discolourations should not be confused with similar symptoms shown when young plants are plagued by severe aphid infestations. A soil value of 36 mg kg -1 (citric acid) or 24 mg kg -1 (Bray 1) serves as point of departure. At lower or higher soil analyses, adjustments are made, taking into account the factors that determine yield potential. Maintenance fertilization of >10 kg P ha -1 is recommended. In cereal crop rotation systems, applications may be adjusted downwards by 30%. Table 5.5.1.3. Phosphorus fertilization guidelines for canola in a legume crop rotation system Potassium The canola plant requires potassium in similar quantities to nitrogen. Unlike other macro-nutrients, most potassium occurs in solution in cell sap. Potassium is an important enzyme activator. It is also important in controlling water losses by regulating the stomas through which plants transpire, and in absorbing and transporting other nutrients, such as nitrogen. Even though potassium deficiencies generally lead to a decrease in vigour, and severe deficiencies may cause yellowing around leaf edges and between the veins of older leaves, potassium deficiencies are usually non-symptomatic in canola, and are therefore easily confused with other deficiency symptoms. As a guideline, the potassium content of soil should be approximately 80 mg kg -1 for heavy-textured soil, and 60 mg kg -1 for light-textured soil. According to this guideline, potassium fertilization will not be required very often. At lower analyses, potassium is applied as for wheat. If the fertilizer is band- placed, an excessively high salt load may cause damage, and it is thus recommended that potassium be broadcast and mixed into the soil. Table 5.5.1.4. Potassium fertilization guidelines following soil analysis Sulphur Sulphur forms part of the structural and enzymatic components of plants. Sulphur is a key component of two essential amino acids, namely cysteine and methionine, and is important for the synthesis of proteins and chlorophyll. Sulphur is also one of the building blocks of glucosinolates, which particularly occur in the growth points and roots of canola plants, and have a suppressive effect on some soil-borne diseases. During the vegetative stage, sulphur deficiencies are characterised by a light-green colour on the youngest leaves, as well as smaller leaves with edges curling upwards to form “cups’’. During the reproductive phase, flowers have a lighter colour and pollen production is impaired. Sulphur deficiencies cause both fewer pods per plant and fewer seeds per pod. Pods are often purple and flattened. The sulphur requirements of canola are approximately four times those of wheat. Therefore, the sulphur fertilization programme requires special attention. As, in some instances, sulphur suppresses the absorption of boron and molybdenum due to an antagonistic effect, these elements will also require attention where high levels of sulphur fertilization are applied. For sulphur supplementation in canola, the guidelines in Table 5.5.1.5 can be used. The time of sampling is important (shortly before planting), since the S content of soils often varies in the course of the season. The carbon content provides a relatively good indication of the sulphur status of soils, with values of <1% indicating possible suboptimal levels. For optimal production, sulphur should preferably be applied in combination with nitrogen as topdressing during the growth season, in an N:S ratio of approximately 7:1. The latter is particularly important on highly leached soils with a carbon content of <1%. On soil with a history of sulphur deficiencies, or where a soil analysis indicates such a condition, sulphur should be applied at 10–15 kg S ha -1 per ton of yield target as a soil application through gypsum (CaSO 4 ), superphosphate, potassium sulphate or sulphur-containing nitrogen fertilizers. It is important to note that plants can only absorb sulphur in the water-soluble sulphate form (SO 4 ). Table 5.5.1.5. Guidelines for sulphur fertilization according to sulphur content of soils Other plant nutrients Canola is sensitive to boron and molybdenum deficiencies. Molybdenum deficiencies are mostly encountered in soil with a low pH, as these soils cause molybdenum to become inaccessible. Deficiencies can be prevented by increasing the soil pH through liming. In molybdenum-deficient soil, deficiencies can be counteracted by applying sodium molybdate (100–150 g ha -1 ) as foliar sprays. The practice of seed treatment with molybdenum is not recommended, since this method usually does not succeed in applying sufficient molybdenum. Boron deficiencies are generally counteracted by applying sodium tetraborate (1–1.5 kg ha -1 ) as a foliar spray during stem elongation (70–80 days after emergence). Soil application of boron can impair germination of canola seed if there is contact with the seed. Thus, soil application of boron in the canola year is generally not recommended. If low boron soil content (<0,5 mg kg -1 ) is suspected or confirmed by a soil analysis, boron may however be band-placed at planting as part of the fertilizer mix or through the use of products where fertilizer pellets are coated in boron (and other trace elements). Such soil applications should only occur when recommended by an expert, and will in most cases only supplement (not replace) the foliar spray. In general, foliar sprays with boron should be regarded as a standard application. The copper, zinc and manganese requirements of canola are higher than those of wheat. Certain factors, such as soil texture, pH, liming, leaching, high carbon content and soil colour, can often be used as an indication of possible deficiencies. The higher figures are used in the case of soils with a higher potential. Analyses should be assessed in terms of vigour and possible spray residues, and in consultation with a plant nutrition expert. Table 5.5.1.6. Soil analysis norms for trace elements currently used for canola (ppm or mg kg -1 , EDTA soluble) Table 5.5.1.7. Leaf analysis norms for canola during the rosette stage (30–40 days after emergence) (Source: Interpretation of Plant Analysis, CSIRO Publishing, PO Box 1139, Collingwood VIC 3066, Australia.) Supplementing trace elements (also known as micro-elements) Micro-elements are needed when an analysis shows the soil to be “low” or “deficient”. Trace element deficiencies can be supplemented by foliar sprays or soil applications. The residual effect of soil applications usually lasts much longer than that of foliar sprays. In calcareous soils with a high pH, manganese in the soil becomes inaccessible to plants, and will have to be applied as a foliar spray. Important aspects to keep in mind for successful trace element supplementation are as follows: Deficiencies should be correctly identified. Plants should be growing actively (not be under moisture stress) and the leaf area should be sufficient. Plants should be dry and the weather should not be very hot during application. After foliar spraying, at least 2–3 hours should pass before there is rain or irrigation, to ensure sufficient absorption by the plant. The manufacturer’s specifications should be strictly followed to ensure success. Trace elements should not be mixed with herbicides. If mixtures of trace elements are used, the lower dose of the various elements should be used in order to prevent an excessively high salt load, which can cause leaf scorch. Thorough wetting of the plant is very important for the best results. Table 5.5.1.8. Trace element recommendations for canola REFERENCES CSIRO PUBLISHING, 1997. Plant Analysis: An Interpretation Manual . D. Reuter & J.B. Robinson (Eds). 2nd Edition. Collingwood, Victoria, Australia. PROTEïENNAVORSINGSTIGTING (PNS), 2014. Canola Production Guide . 2nd Revised Edition. South Africa.
tables: Phosphorus status of the soil (mg kg -1 )
Deficient: S application above specific crop requirement (>15-20 S ha -1 )
Sufficient: S application at maintenance level (15 kg S ha -1 )
More than sufficient: S application below maintenance level (10 kg S ha -1 )
